title: Fitting a door frame liner
subtitle: Box out a structural doorway opening with a planted timber frame
excerpt: How to fit a door liner (planted lining frame) in an opening that has been formed in a wall: measuring the opening, cutting the head and leg pieces, squaring and plumbing the frame, packing the reveals, and fixing ready for a door to be hung.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A planted timber frame that lines the inside faces of a doorway opening, providing a finished planed surface for the door stop and architrave and the hinge and latch rebates for the door. Also called a lining set or planted lining. The liner is not structural; the lintel or trimmer over the opening carries the load.
A narrow timber strip planted (nailed) onto the door liner face against which the door closes. Distinct from a door stop fitting; this is the timber rebate the door closes against.

content.text: is fitted in a new or altered opening before the door is hung. The liner provides the finished reveal surfaces and the planted
that the door closes against. The opening must have a sound lintel above it before the liner is fitted.

Measure the width and height of the opening at several points. The opening should be at least 25 mm wider and 25 mm taller than the door to allow the frame and door stop to fit with clearance for adjustment. Measure the wall thickness: the liner width should equal the wall thickness so the liner face sits flush to the plaster on both sides.

Cut the head piece (top horizontal) to the full internal width of the opening plus the thickness of both leg pieces. Cut both leg pieces (verticals) to the full height of the opening minus the head piece thickness. Check all cuts are square. On a pre-made liner set, the head piece sits over the top of the legs and is screwed from above; on a site-made set, the head can sit between the legs or over them.
Step 3, fit and pack the liner

Offer the assembled liner into the opening. Pack behind the legs and head with timber packing pieces (pairs of timber wedges work well) to bring the liner plumb and square. The liner must be plumb on the face and square in plan: check with the spirit level and a large square. Screw through the liner into the masonry or stud frame at 600 mm intervals, driving screws at an angle through the face to reach the structure behind, or through the back of the liner into packing blocks fixed to the masonry.
Step 4, fix the door stop and trim

Nail the door stop to the liner face once the door is temporarily hung and the stop position confirmed. The stop should hold the door flush with the architrave face when closed. Fit the architrave on both sides to cover the gap between the liner and the plaster, mitreing the head and legs at 45 degrees.

attrs.symptom: Liner not sitting flush to the plaster on both faces
attrs.cause: The wall is not uniform in thickness, or the liner was cut to the wrong width.
attrs.fix: Pack behind the face that sits proud to push it flush. If the liner is too wide, plane the back face to reduce the width.

attrs.symptom: Door drops or binds after hanging
attrs.cause: The liner is not plumb or the head is not level.
attrs.fix: Check the liner with a spirit level, adjust the packing, re-check, and tighten the fixings before hanging the door.
